coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots-coated hierarchical CuO microspheres composite for use as binder-free anode for lithium-ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Kim, Jongmin
Jang, Wooree
Kim, Ji Hoon
Yang, Cheol-Min
coredata.dc:description: There is an urgent need to develop improved anode materials for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs). Herein, we report the synthesis of a graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-coated hierarchical nanoflake-based CuO microspheres (H–CuO) composite on Cu foam via a one-pot hydrothermal technique for use as a binder-free anode for LIBs. The carboxyl-functionalized GQD coating on H–CuO not only results in lower charge-transfer resistance and enhanced electrical conductivity but also prevents the dissolution and agglomeration of the electrode. The GQDs/H–CuO composite anode exhibits a reversible capacity as high as 609 mAh g−1 (pristine H–CuO: 61 mAh g−1) after 200 cycles at 0.2 A g−1. It also shows long-term cycling stability, exhibiting a capacity retention rate of 79.4% after 1000 cycles (pristine H–CuO: 0.7%) at a high current density (2 A g−1) and improved initial coulombic efficiency at 88.2% (pristine H–CuO: 75.2%). The superior electrochemical properties of the GQDs/H–CuO composite anode are attributable to the graphene networks, which help maintain a high specific surface area and effectively protect the anodic active material from forming an unstable solid electrolyte interface layer. The proposed strategy for fabricating the GQD-coated metal oxide microsphere-based anode should contribute to the development of next-generation LIBs with improved electrochemical performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion batteries
CuO microspheres
Graphene quantum dots
Binder-free anodes

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST) Institutional Program and the Nano-Material Technology Development Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) , funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT ( 2016M3A7B4027695 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of process for fabricating GQDs/H–CuO composite film electrode via hydrothermal method. FE-SEM images of (b) pristine H–CuO and (d) GQDs/H–CuO composite films and (c, e) corresponding higher-magnification images.
(a) HR-TEM image, (b) SAED pattern, and (c–g) SEM-EDS mapping results for GQDs/H–CuO composite film.

Initial four CV curves of (a) pristine H–CuO and (b) GQDs/H–CuO composite anodes at scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1. GCD profiles of (c) pristine H–CuO and (d) GQDs/H–CuO composite anodes at 0.1 A g−1. Rate capabilities of (e) pristine H–CuO and (f) GQDs/H–CuO composite anodes at different current densities (0.1–5.0 A g−1).
Cycling performances and coulombic efficiencies of pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite anodes at current densities of (a, b) 0.2 A g−1 and (c, d) 2 A g−1.

(a) b-values of pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite anodes as functions of CV potential (cathodic sweeps). (b) Relative contributions of capacitive and diffusion-controlled processes to charge storage in pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite anodes at various scan rates. CV curves of (c) pristine H–CuO and (d) GQDs/H–CuO composite anodes and calculated capacitive contributions (shaded regions) at scan rate 2 mV s−1. Nyquist plots of pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite anodes (e) before and (f) after 200 cycles (equivalent circuits are shown in insets).
Comparison of fitted parameters obtained from Nyquist plots for pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite anodes.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots-coated hierarchical CuO microspheres composite for use as binder-free anode for lithium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Advanced Composite Materials, Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST), Chudong-ro 92, Bongdong-eup, Wanju-gun, Jeollabuk-do, 55324, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Advanced Composite Materials, Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST), Chudong-ro 92, Bongdong-eup, Wanju-gun, Jeollabuk-do, 55324, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: There is an urgent need to develop improved anode materials for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs). Herein, we report the synthesis of a graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-coated hierarchical nanoflake-based CuO microspheres (H–CuO) composite on Cu foam via a one-pot hydrothermal technique for use as a binder-free anode for LIBs. The carboxyl-functionalized GQD coating on H–CuO not only results in lower charge-transfer resistance and enhanced electrical conductivity but also prevents the dissolution and agglomeration of the electrode. The GQDs/H–CuO composite anode exhibits a reversible capacity as high as 609 mAh g−1 (pristine H–CuO: 61 mAh g−1) after 200 cycles at 0.2 A g−1. It also shows long-term cycling stability, exhibiting a capacity retention rate of 79.4% after 1000 cycles (pristine H–CuO: 0.7%) at a high current density (2 A g−1) and improved initial coulombic efficiency at 88.2% (pristine H–CuO: 75.2%). The superior electrochemical properties of the GQDs/H–CuO composite anode are attributable to the graphene networks, which help maintain a high specific surface area and effectively protect the anodic active material from forming an unstable solid electrolyte interface layer. The proposed strategy for fabricating the GQD-coated metal oxide microsphere-based anode should contribute to the development of next-generation LIBs with improved electrochemical performance.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the rapid depletion of nonrenewable fossil resources and the environmental problems arising from their use, there is an increasing demand for more efficient, sustainable, and clean energy sources [1]. Rechargeable lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are considered the best power sources for various consumer portable electronic devices because of their high energy density, low self-discharge capacity, high open-circuit voltage, and environmental benignity [2–4]. In commercial LIBs, graphite-based carbonaceous materials are generally used as the anode active material because of their excellent electrochemical conductivity, low cost, and high charge/discharge stability [5,6]. However, graphite has a relatively low theoretical capacity at 372 mAh g−1. Therefore, significant efforts have been devoted to develop advanced anodic materials with higher capacity for LIBs [7–9].

Transition metal oxides, such as copper oxide (CuO), NiO, ZnO, SnO2, Fe3O4, MnO2, and Co3O4, have been explored extensively and are employed as electrode materials in energy conversion and storage devices owing to their ability to undergo fast and reversible redox reactions [10,11]. Among them, CuO is a promising anode material for LIB applications because of its relatively larger theoretical capacity (674 mAh g−1), natural abundance, low cost, and environmental friendliness [12–14]. However, the practical applicability of CuO as an anode material in LIBs has been plagued by its low electrical conductivity, rapid capacity fading, and poor cycling stability [15]. To overcome these drawbacks, considerable efforts have been made to develop CuO micro-/nanostructures with different morphologies such as nanowires, nanoporous structures, nanosheets, and pompon-like hierarchical hollow microspheres [16–19]. Compared with bulk CuO anodes, micro-/nanostructured CuO anodes show improved electrochemical performance. However, another critical issue is that these materials show low initial coulombic efficiency. Therefore, they must be coated with a carbon material with higher stability, as this allows for both surface functionalization and improved structural stability.

In this study, we report a simple but effective method for fabricating a graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-coated hierarchical nanoflake-based CuO microspheres (H–CuO) composite. The composite shows significantly improved electrochemical properties and thus is suitable for use as an anode material for LIBs. We believe that the proposed electrode design strategy has the following advantages:

(a) It is an effective strategy for fabricating binder-free electrodes using three-dimensional (3D) Cu foam. Cu foam exhibits high porosity, a large specific surface area, good permeability, excellent electrical conductivity, and good strain accommodation characteristics. Films comprising arrays of CuO microspheres were grown directly on 3D Cu foam for use as binder-free, free-standing anodes, thus eliminating the need for a polymeric binder (e.g., polytetrafluoroethylene and polyvinylidene fluoride) and a conductive carbon additive (e.g., acetylene black and Super-P). Therefore, binder-free electrodes avoid the problem of binder decomposition during the electrochemical process as well as effectively decrease the weight of the LIBs [20].

(b) The unique micro-/nanostructure of H–CuO results in enhanced electrochemical performance. The decrease in the distance for Li+ ion transport results in accelerated ion transfer, as per the following equation [21]:
(1)
t =
L 2
/ D
where t is the diffusion time of the Li+ ions, L is the diffusion length, and D is the diffusion coefficient. The two-dimensional (2D) nanoflake arrays used in the proposed anode design not only have the ability to store energy at a high rate owing to the short diffusion length for the Li+ ions but also facilitate electron transport through a continuous pathway, resulting in improved charge-transfer kinetics [22]. However, in practical applications, using CuO as the LIB anode suffers from large volume expansion (174%) and gradual pulverization during repeated cycling processes, thus leading to loss of electrical contact with the current collector. Therefore, the hierarchically structured microspheres result in a large electrode/electrolyte interfacial contact area and internal void space, both of which are beneficial for improving the capacity and effectively accommodating the volume expansion of the electrode [23,24].

(c) The GQDs boost the electrochemical performance of the composite. Various carbon-based materials, such as graphene, graphene aerogel, GQD, pitch-derived carbon, biomass-derived carbon, have been widely adopted as anode materials to replace or improve graphite-based anodes [2,25,26]. Among the various carbon-based nanomaterials available, GQDs have attracted considerable attention for use in several applications, such as supercapacitors, solar cells, photoelectrochemical water splitting, sensors, and light-emitting diodes. In particular, they are considered highly promising as anode materials for LIBs [27–32]. With respect to LIBs, GQDs help to improve the electrochemical performance owing to their excellent electrical conductivity, large specific surface area, high mobility, and promote ion transport [33]. In particular, it is expected that the GQD coating would help minimize the agglomeration of the CuO microspheres and decrease the electrochemical impedance.

Herein, we report the direct synthesis of GQD/H–CuO composite on Cu foam via a simple hydrothermal method. The binder-free GQDs/H–CuO anode showed enhanced reversible capacity (609 mAh g−1 at 0.2 A g−1 after 200 cycles) as well as good rate reversibility and an extremely high capacity retention rate (~79.4% after 1000 cycles at a current density as high as 2 A g−1).

H–CuO films were synthesized directly on Cu foam substrates using a one-step hydrothermal process. Prior to the synthesis process, the Cu foam was cleaned by ultrasonication in acetone, ethanol, and deionized (DI) water to remove surface impurities. Next, 2.5 M NaOH (Sigma-Aldrich, USA) and 0.125 M (NH4)2S2O8 (Daejung Chemical, Korea) were dissolved in DI water (50 mL) under stirring for 30 min at room temperature. Then, 20 mg of a powder of carboxyl-functionalized GQDs (~10 nm particle size, XFNANO, China) was added to the solution and dissolved in it (this step was skipped in the case of the coatings without the GQDs). To evaluate the effect of the contents of GQD coating on the surface of H–CuO, different amounts of GQDs (10 and 30 mg) were also added during preparation. The mixed solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, and the cleaned Cu foam was immersed and kept in the reaction solution at 150 °C for 6 h. To study the effect of hydrothermal reaction time on morphology of GQDs/H–CuO composite film, the hydrothermal reactions were also performed for 2 and 10 h. Next, the sample was rinsed with ethanol and DI water several times and dried at 60 °C for 12 h in an oven. Finally, pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite films were obtained after annealing at 400 °C in an Ar atmosphere for 2 h. The mass loadings of active materials were precisely determined from the mass difference before and after the hydrothermal process using a highly sensitive balance (GR-202, A&D, Japan) with 0.01 mg accuracy. The densities of the pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite films were measured as 1.7756 and 1.9941 g cm−3, respectively, by using a high precision pycnometer (BELPycon, MicrotracBEL, Japan).

The morphologies of the pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite films were observed using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM; Verios 460 L, FEI, Hillsboro, OR, USA) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM; Tecnai G2 F20, FEI, Hillsboro, OR, USA). To investigate the crystal structures and chemical states of the samples, X-ray diffraction (XRD; Rigaku SmartLab, Tokyo, Japan) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; K-Alpha, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) analyses were performed.

To evaluate the electrochemical performance of the samples, standard CR2032-type coin cells were assembled inside an Ar-filled glove box (Korea Kiyon, Korea) using Li metal as the counter/reference electrode. The as-fabricated pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite films were cut into circular disks with a diameter of 15 mm and used as working electrodes without any binder or additives. Solutions of 1 M LiPF6 dissolved in ethylene carbonate/dimethyl carbonate (1:1 v/v) and porous polyethylene membrane were used as the electrolyte and separator, respectively. The battery performances of the prepared pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite anodes were studied using a battery test system (WBCS3000, Won-A Tech, Korea). The cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were performed for potentials of 0.005–3.0 V (versus Li/Li+) at various scan rates while the galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) measurements were performed at different current densities. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis was carried out using an electrochemical workstation (ZIVE SP2, Won-A Tech, Korea) for frequencies between 100 kHz and 10 mHz.

A schematic of the process for fabricating the binder-free GQDs/H–CuO composite film on Cu foam substrates is shown in Fig. 1 a. The H–CuO film was grown directly on the Cu foam, which had a 3D porous structure, via a hydrothermal process. The surface of the Cu foam was effectively oxidized in a mixed-alkaline solution (NaOH and (NH4)2S2O8, used as an oxidant) and coated uniformly with CuO nanoflakes (Fig. S1b). The chemical reaction involved is as follows [34]:
(2) Cu + 4NaOH + (NH4)2S2O8 → CuO + 2Na2SO4 + 2NH3 + 3H2O

During the hydrothermal process, the CuO nanoflakes gradually undergo self-assembly, leading to the formation of H–CuO. Carboxyl-functionalized GQDs have a strong affinity for metal oxides. This facilitates the formation of a coating of the GQDs on the surface of H–CuO [35]. For an addition of 20 mg GQDs, the increased weight after the GQD coating on the surface of H–CuO film was 40 μg (~1.7 wt% GQD loading). In addition, the mass loadings of the pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite films were 1.27 and 1.30 mg cm−2, respectively. The morphology of the films could be controlled by tuning the hydrothermal reaction time. When the hydrothermal reaction time decreased from 6 to 2 h, small CuO nanoflakes and a small quantity of H–CuO are observed (Fig. S2a). As the reaction time increased to 10 h, the CuO nanoflakes agglomerated together and bulk structures are formed (Fig. S2b).

The phase purities and crystallinities of the pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite films were characterized by XRD analysis. As shown in Fig. S3a, both the pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite films showed diffraction peaks corresponding to the monoclinic CuO phase with lattice constants of a = 4.6853 Å, b = 3.4257 Å, c = 5.1303 Å, and β = 99.549° (JCPDS card no. 45–0937) and cubic Cu (JCPDS card no. 04–0836). Peaks related to other impurity phases such as Cu2O and Cu(OH)2 were not detected, indicating the successful formation of pure CuO. Meanwhile, characteristic diffraction peaks related to the GQDs were also not detected owing to the low GQD content and relatively low diffraction intensity.

The surface morphologies and microstructures of the pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite films were observed by FE-SEM. Fig. S1a shows the surface morphology of the Cu foam substrate as a current collector. As shown in Fig. 1b–e, the FE-SEM images of the pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite films clearly indicate the formation of microspheres (average diameter of approximately 8 μm) packed with nanoflakes (Figs. S1c and d). In both cases, the nanoflakes exhibited an average thickness of ~96 nm, width of ~375 nm, and length of ~1.9 μm. However, after the formation of the GQD coating, the GQDs could not be observed on H–CuO through FE-SEM owing their extremely small size [36]. Therefore, to analyze the microstructural characteristics of the GQDs/H–CuO composite film, HR-TEM was performed. Fig. 2 a shows a typical HR-TEM image of the GQDs/H–CuO composite film. GQDs with an average diameter of approximately 8.2 nm could be observed clearly. The interplanar spacings (d-spacings) for the (002) planes of the GQDs and CuO were determined to be 0.33 and 0.35 nm, respectively [37,38]. Furthermore, the selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern confirmed the presence of GQDs in the GQDs/H–CuO composite film (Fig. 2b). The chemical state of the surface of the GQDs/H–CuO composite film was analyzed using XPS. In the Cu 2p XPS spectrum, two primary peaks were observed, at 933.8 and 953.8 eV. These could be assigned to Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2, respectively, with the spin-energy separation of 20.0 eV indicating the presence of Cu2+ ions in CuO (Fig. S3b) [39]. Furthermore, shake-up satellite peaks (941.3, 943.7, and 962.3 eV) were also observed, which indicated the presence of pure CuO [40]. The C 1s XPS spectrum could be deconvoluted into four peaks, at 284.8, 285.7, 288.1, and 289.1 eV, which corresponded to C─C/C═C, C─O, C═O, and COOH bonds, respectively, indicating the presence of a graphitic carbon structure with a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups (Fig. S3c) [41]. In addition, the results of the SEM-energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mapping analysis of the GQDs/H–CuO composite film further confirmed the presence of elemental Cu, O, and C (Fig. 2c–g).

To evaluate the electrochemical performances of the pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite films as binder-free anodes for LIBs, we systematically performed CV, GCD, and EIS measurements. Fig. 3 a and b shows the CV curves of the pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite anodes for the first four cycles at 0.1 mV s−1. During the first cathodic scan (lithiation process) of the GQDs/H–CuO composite anode, the three peaks located at 2.15, 1.14, and 0.71 V were attributable to the transformation of CuO into CuⅡ 1-x CuⅠ
x O1-x/2, Cu2O, and Cu, respectively, as shown in the multistep electrochemical reactions given below [42]:
(3) CuO + xLi+ + xe− → CuⅡ 1−x CuⅠ
x O1−x/2 + x/2Li2O
(4) CuⅡ 1−x CuⅠ
x O1−x/2 + (1 – x)Li+ + (1 – x)e– → 1/2Cu2O + (1 – x)/2Li2O
(5) 1/2Cu2O + Li+ + e– → Cu + 1/2Li2O

In addition, the peak at 0.71 V was related to the irreversible formation of a solid electrolyte interface (SEI) layer over the surface of the electrode during the discharge process [43]. During the subsequent anodic scan (delithiation process), the three anodic peaks at 1.50, 2.49, and 2.76 V corresponded to the oxidation of Cu to Cu2O and then to CuO. The intensities of the cathodic peaks of the pristine H–CuO anode decreased significantly during the second cycle, whereas those of the GQDs/H–CuO composite anode almost overlapped from the first to the fourth cycle. These results confirmed that the hybridization of the GQDs with H–CuO greatly enhanced the reversibility of the electrochemical reactions as well as the cycling stability of the anode material [44].

Fig. 3c and d shows the initial five GCD profiles of the pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite anodes at 0.1 A g−1. During the first discharge process, both anodes exhibited three decreasing-potential ranges (2.40–1.50, 1.50–0.80, and 0.80–0.01 V), which corresponded to the multistep reactions for the reduction of CuO to Cu. During the first charge process, three voltage plateaus were observed, at 0.01–2.29, 2.29–2.52, and 2.52–3.00 V. These were associated with the gradual oxidation of Cu and were consistent with the CV results discussed previously. During the subsequent cycles, the observed charge/discharge curves of the GQDs/H–CuO composite anode overlapped significantly and were more stable than those of the pristine H–CuO anode. This was indicative of the excellent reversibility and low electrochemical polarization of the active material of the former [45]. Moreover, while the pristine H–CuO anode exhibited a higher initial discharge capacity, it underwent capacity fading to a greater degree as compared with the GQDs/H–CuO composite anode. The initial discharge/charge capacities of the pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite anodes were 2264/1703 and 2195/1936 mAh g−1, respectively, and decreased to 1274/1272 and 1575/1586 mAh g−1, respectively, after five cycles. From these results, it can be concluded that the GQDs/H–CuO composite anode exhibited a higher initial coulombic efficiency (88.2%) as compared with that of the pristine H–CuO anode (75.2%). This is because the GQDs on the surface of H–CuO limited direct contact between the electrode and the electrolyte and thus prevented the formation of a thick SEI layer [46].

The rate capabilities of the pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite anodes were evaluated through GCD measurements performed at different current densities (0.1–5.0 A g−1), as shown in Fig. 3e and f. For both anodes, the specific charge/discharge capacity decreased gradually with an increase in the current density. This was attributable to the nonlinear behavior of Li+ ion diffusion at the electrode/electrolyte interface [47]. The average discharge capacities of the pristine H–CuO anode at current densities of 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, and 5.0 A g−1 were 1547, 1052, 793, 434, 99, and 36 mAh g−1, respectively. On the other hand, the GQDs/H–CuO composite anode exhibited higher average discharge capacities at 1702, 1469, 1277, 977, 596, and 184 mAh g−1, respectively, for the same current densities. In particular, the capacity retention rates of the pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite anodes were 61.7% (955 mAh g−1) and 75.6% (1287 mAh g−1), respectively, when the current density was reduced to 0.1 A g−1. These results suggest that the GQD coating enhanced the rate capability of H–CuO because the GQDs acted as a conducting and buffering medium, thus improving electron/electrolyte transport on the surface of the active material.

The cycling performances of the pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite anodes were evaluated at a low current density of 0.2 A g−1 for 200 cycles, as shown in Fig. 4 a and b. The specific charge/discharge capacities of the pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite anodes during the first cycle were observed to be 921/1144 and 1058/1245 mAh g−1, respectively, which corresponded to initial coulombic efficiencies of 80.5% and 84.9%, respectively. After 200 cycles, the discharge capacity of the pristine H–CuO anode decreased rapidly to 61 mAh g−1 (retention rate of 5.3%), while the GQDs/H–CuO composite anode continued to exhibit a discharge capacity of 609 mAh g−1 and thus showed relatively slower capacity fading (retention rate of 48.9%) (Fig. S4a). These results were compared with those for previously reported carbon-supported CuO-based anode materials (Table S1). The comparison confirmed the superior cycling performance of the GQDs/H–CuO composite anode fabricated in this study. Moreover, the coulombic efficiency of the GQDs/H–CuO composite anode was more stable than that of the pristine CuO anode. The morphological and structural changes in the pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite anodes after 200 cycles were investigated using FE-SEM. The pristine H–CuO anode showed the formation of a thicker SEI layer, along with cracks and agglomeration (Fig. S5a), whereas for the GQDs/H–CuO composite anode, the microsphere structure was well maintained because of the formation of a stable SEI layer (Fig. S5b). In general, an irreversible capacity loss and low coulombic efficiency can be attributed to the continuous decomposition of the electrolyte and the formation of an unstable SEI layer at the electrode/electrolyte interface during the lithiation/delithiation processes [48,49]. However, during the subsequent cycles, the GQDs/H–CuO composite anode exhibited substantially improved electrochemical performance compared with that of the pristine H–CuO anode. This is because the GQDs on the surface of H–CuO promoted the hopping of the Li+ ions through the adjacent carboxylic sites and provided short diffusion pathways for the ions at the electrode/electrolyte interface [50]. To analyze the effect of the contents of GQD coating on the H–CuO surface, different amounts of GQDs (10 and 30 mg) were added during the preparation of GQDs/H–CuO composites. The increased weights after the formation of the GQD coating were 10 (~0.4 wt% GQD loading) and 70 μg (~3.0 wt% GQD loading) for addition of 10 and 30 mg GQDs, respectively. Overall, the GQDs/H–CuO composite anodes improved cycling performance compared to the pristine H–CuO anode because of the shielding effect of the GQDs (Fig. S6). However, the GQDs/H–CuO composite anode with highest GQD content had a negative effect on the capacity after 200 cycles because excessive GQDs blocked the transport of Li+ ions [51].

The long-term cycling stabilities of the pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite anodes were further evaluated at a high current density of 2 A g−1 for 1000 cycles, as shown in Fig. 4c and d. The pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite anodes showed initial charge/discharge capacities of 643/685 and 737/768 mAh g−1, respectively, with corresponding coulombic efficiencies of 93.8 and 95.9%. After 1000 cycles, the discharge capacity of the pristine H–CuO anode decreased sharply to 5.3 mAh g−1, and its capacity retention rate was ~0.7%. This was due to the side reactions arising from the large electrode/electrolyte interfacial contact area because of the H–CuO microspheres (Fig. S4b) [52]. In contrast, while the GQDs/H–CuO composite anode exhibited capacity fading during the first 50 cycles, its discharge capacity was 610 mAh g−1 and capacity retention rate was ~79.4% after 1000 cycles. The decrease of capacity during the initial cycles mainly results from the irreversible decomposition of the electrolyte [53]. Notably, the increased capacity was primarily because of the reversible formation of a polymeric gel-like film (e.g., SEI layer) owing to the electrochemical catalytic activity of the GQDs [54–56]. These results confirmed that the presence of the GQDs on the H–CuO anode resulted in dramatic improvements in its reversible Li storage capacity and cycling stability.

To further elucidate the significantly enhanced electrochemical performance of the GQDs/H–CuO composite electrode and the kinetics of its Li+ ion storage behavior, the CV curves of the two electrodes were measured at various scan rates (0.1–2.0 mV s−1) (Figs. S7a and b). The relationship between the measured current (i) and scan rate (v) obeys the following power law [57,58]:
(6)
i = a ×
v b
where a is an adjustable parameter and b is the slope of the log(v)–log(i) plot. The log(v)–log(i) plots for the pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite anodes for various potentials are shown in Figs. S7c and d. A b-value of 0.5 indicates a diffusion-controlled process, whereas a value of 1.0 indicates a surface capacitive-controlled process [59]. Fig. 5 a shows the b-values for potentials between 0.5 and 2.0 V (versus Li/Li+) during the cathodic sweeps (extracted from Figs. S7c and d). The calculated b-values for the various potential values were larger than 0.5, indicating that the charge-storage kinetics were related to both diffusion- and surface-controlled redox reactions. In particular, the b-values of the pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite anodes were 0.94 and 1.04, respectively, at 1.5 V in the cathodic region, suggesting capacitive-dominated charge-storage behavior because of a surface Faradaic redox reaction [60]. Overall, the GQDs/H–CuO composite anode exhibited relatively higher b-values than those of the pristine H–CuO anode. This confirmed that the former had a higher specific capacity and faster reaction kinetics.

For further quantitative analysis of the contribution of the surface capacitive (k 1 v) and diffusion-controlled processes (k 2 v 1/2) at given potentials, the current response i (V) at a fixed potential was determined using the following equation [61]:
(7)
i
( V )
=
k 1
v +
k 2
v
1 / 2
where k 1 and k 2 are adjustable parameters. For analytical purposes, this equation can be rearranged as
(8)
i
( V )
/
v
1 / 2
=
k 1
v
1 / 2
+
k 2

By plotting v 1/2 versus i/v 1/2, the values of coefficients k 1 and k 2 could be obtained from the slope and intercept, respectively, of the straight line (Figs. S8a and b). Thus, in this manner, we were able to quantitatively determine the current contributions at specific fixed potentials for varying scan rates. Fig. 5b presents the calculated contribution ratio for the pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite anodes at various scan rates. This electrochemical kinetics analysis revealed that the capacitive contribution increased gradually with an increase in the scan rate, indicating that the surface-dominated pseudocapacitive behavior was the primary energy-storage mechanism with respect to the overall capacity. In particular, it was observed that, for all the scan rates, the capacitive contribution of the GQDs/H–CuO composite anode was higher than that of the pristine H–CuO anode. This confirmed that the GQD coating on the anode surface provided efficient electron-transfer pathways and resulted in a fast surface capacitive reaction during the electrochemical process. This is the reason the GQDs/H–CuO composite anode showed significantly enhanced rate performance and improved cycling stability as compared with those of the pristine H–CuO anode [62,63]. The contributions of the surface capacitive (shaded region) and diffusion-controlled processes (unshaded region) to the CV curves at the scan rates of 0.1 and 2.0 mV s−1 are shown in Figs. S8c and d and Fig. 5c and d. The large surface capacitive contribution of the GQDs/H–CuO composite anode can be attributed to its higher electrical conductivity, more stable surface chemistry, and rapid redox reaction kinetics, which, in turn, were ascribed to the GQD coating [64].

To further elucidate the reason for the improvements in the Li storage performance of the GQDs/H–CuO composite anode with respect to that of the pristine H–CuO anode, EIS measurements were performed before and after the stability tests. Fig. 5e and f shows the Nyquist plots of the pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite anodes as measured before and after 200 GCD cycles at a current density of 0.2 A g−1. The obtained impedance data were fitted using equivalent electrical circuits (insets in Fig. 5e and f), and the fitting results are presented in Table 1 . Before cycling, the Nyquist plots for both anodes consisted of a semicircle in the high-frequency region, which corresponded to the charge-transfer resistance (R ct), and a straight line in the low-frequency region, which was related to the Warburg impedance (Z w). Here, R s and CPE are the solution resistance and constant phase element, respectively. The GQDs/H–CuO composite anode exhibited a lower R ct value than that of the pristine H–CuO anode, suggesting that the attachment of the GQDs onto H–CuO sped up charge transfer between the anode and the electrolyte because the GQDs enhanced the electronic conductivity [65]. After 200 cycles, two semicircles were observed in the Nyquist plots of the pristine H–CuO and GQDs/H–CuO composite anodes. The first semicircle, which was located in the high-frequency region, was ascribed to the resistance of the SEI layer (R sei), while the second semicircle, which was in the mid-frequency region, corresponded to R ct [66]. Clearly, the R sei and R ct values of the GQDs/H–CuO composite anode were 65.6 and 30.7 Ω, respectively, and were lower than those of the pristine H–CuO anode (71.9 and 96.9 Ω, respectively). These results confirmed that the addition of the GQDs effectively enhanced the ionic/electronic conductivity and ensured the formation of a more stable SEI layer on the H–CuO anode surface during cycling, thus resulting in outstanding electrochemical performance [67–69].

In summary, we fabricated a GQDs/H–CuO composite film electrode for use as a binder-free integrated anode via a simple hydrothermal technique and subsequently investigated its Li storage properties. The GQDs/H–CuO composite anode exhibited a high reversible specific capacity (609 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles at 0.2 A g−1), excellent capacity retention (retention rate of ~79.4% after 1000 cycles at 2 A g−1), and better rate capability as compared with that of the pristine H–CuO anode. These outstanding electrochemical Li storage characteristics are attributable to the synergy between the GQDs and H–CuO, which resulted in a large interfacial contact area with the electrolyte, short ion/electron diffusion pathways, enhanced electrical conductivity, and fast charge-transfer kinetics. The proposed strategy is suitable for developing transition metal oxides suitable for use as high-performance anodes for LIBs and can be applied for designing micro-/nanostructures electrodes for applications such as water electrolysis, supercapacitors, and solar cells.

Jongmin Kim: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Wooree Jang: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis. Ji Hoon Kim: Data curation, Formal analysis. Cheol-Min Yang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Writing - review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
